nodes.name: utc_to_tai_leap_second_kernel
nodes.description: Converts a two-part UTC Julian date to TAI by resolving the applicable leap-second offset. Internally converts Julian date to calendar date (jd2cal) to locate the correct leap-second table entry, then adds the offset to produce the TAI two-part Julian date.

inputs.name: utc1
inputs.type_desc: float — first part of two-part UTC Julian date
inputs.constraints: finite; utc1+utc2 must be within the supported leap-second table range

inputs.name: utc2
inputs.type_desc: float — second part of two-part UTC Julian date
inputs.constraints: finite; together with utc1 represents a valid UTC epoch

outputs.name: tai1
outputs.type_desc: float — first part of two-part TAI Julian date
outputs.constraints: tai1 + tai2 = utc1 + utc2 + leap_seconds/86400

outputs.name: tai2
outputs.type_desc: float — second part of two-part TAI Julian date
outputs.constraints: precision-preserving companion to tai1

nodes.type_signature: (utc1: float — first part of two-part UTC Julian date, utc2: float — second part of two-part UTC Julian date) -> tuple[float — first part of two-part TAI Julian date, float — second part of two-part TAI Julian date]
nodes.conceptual_summary: piecewise_scalar_offset_resolver_via_lookup_table_projection. Given a two-part high-precision scalar encoding of a point on a continuous timeline, this transform projects that scalar into a discrete calendar-coordinate space solely to locate the applicable correction offset within a piecewise-constant lookup table. The resolved offset is then lifted back into the original scalar encoding space and added to the input pair, producing a new two-part scalar that is numerically equivalent to the original plus the correction, while preserving the full floating-point precision of the two-part representation. The operation is a piecewise-constant scalar translation whose correction magnitude is determined by the region of the timeline the input falls within.. Applications: Geodesy / GNSS signal processing: translating between two different epoch-referenced coordinate frames (e.g., GPS time vs. UTC) where the offset between frames accumulates in discrete, historically recorded steps — the exact structural analog of resolving a piecewise-constant cumulative correction indexed by a calendar projection., Thermodynamic calibration pipelines: converting a raw sensor scalar reading to a corrected physical quantity using a piecewise-constant calibration table keyed by temperature or pressure regime, where the applicable correction coefficient is resolved by first projecting the raw reading into a discrete regime index., Compiler / runtime version resolution: mapping a floating-point semantic-version scalar (or integer build timestamp) to the applicable ABI compatibility offset by projecting the version into a discrete range table, then adding the resolved symbol-offset delta to produce a corrected memory address or symbol index — structurally identical piecewise lookup-and-add., Epidemiological cohort studies: adjusting a continuous age or exposure-duration scalar by a piecewise-constant population-risk-stratum offset (resolved via projection of the scalar into a discrete age-band or exposure-band table), producing a corrected risk-adjusted scalar for downstream statistical modeling.

nodes.name: order_utc_parts_by_magnitude
nodes.description: Determines which of the two UTC Julian date parts has greater absolute magnitude and promotes both to a common numeric type, placing the larger-magnitude part first (u1) and the smaller part second (u2) to maximize floating-point precision in subsequent arithmetic.

outputs.name: u1
outputs.constraints: promoted type, |u1| >= |u2|

outputs.name: u2
outputs.constraints: promoted type, |u2| <= |u1|

outputs.name: big1
outputs.constraints: true iff utc1 had the larger magnitude

nodes.type_signature: (utc1: float, utc2: float) -> tuple[float, float, bool]

nodes.name: extract_fractional_day_and_julian_offset
nodes.description: Calls jd2cal on the ordered two-part Julian date to decompose it into calendar fields and extract the sub-day fractional part fd. Also computes z2, the integer-day portion of u2 by subtracting fd, for later reassembly.

outputs.name: fd
outputs.constraints: fractional day in [0, 1)

outputs.name: z2
outputs.constraints: u2 minus fractional day; integer Julian day contribution of u2

nodes.name: lookup_leap_second_offset_today
nodes.description: Queries the leap-seconds table with the Julian date offset from J2000 to obtain the TAI-UTC difference Δt0 applicable at 0h of the current UTC day.

inputs.name: DJ2000
inputs.constraints: constant: Julian date of J2000 epoch

outputs.name: delta_t0
outputs.constraints: TAI minus UTC in seconds at 0h today

nodes.type_signature: (u1: float, u2: float, DJ2000: float) -> float

nodes.name: lookup_leap_second_offset_tomorrow
nodes.description: Queries the leap-seconds table for the TAI-UTC offset at 0h of the next UTC day (tomorrow) to detect whether a leap-second event occurs between today and tomorrow.

inputs.name: DJ2000
inputs.constraints: constant: Julian date of J2000 epoch

outputs.name: delta_t24
outputs.constraints: TAI minus UTC in seconds at 0h tomorrow

nodes.type_signature: (u1: float, u2: float, DJ2000: float) -> float

nodes.name: spread_leap_second_into_fractional_day
nodes.description: Detects a leap-second jump by comparing today's and tomorrow's TAI-UTC offsets and distributes any difference uniformly across the preceding day by adjusting the fractional day fd. This smears the leap second into the 86400 seconds of the current day.

inputs.name: delta_t0
inputs.constraints: leap-second offset at 0h today, in seconds

inputs.name: delta_t24
inputs.constraints: leap-second offset at 0h tomorrow, in seconds

outputs.name: fd_adjusted
outputs.constraints: fractional day adjusted for leap smearing

nodes.type_signature: (fd: float, delta_t0: float, delta_t24: float) -> float

nodes.name: assemble_tai_second_part
nodes.description: Combines the integer-day component z2, the leap-adjusted fractional day fd_adjusted, and today's leap-second offset delta_t0 (converted to day units) to form the second part of the two-part TAI Julian date a2.

inputs.name: delta_t0
inputs.constraints: TAI-UTC offset in seconds at today 0h

outputs.name: a2
outputs.constraints: second part of TAI Julian date

nodes.type_signature: (z2: float, fd_adjusted: float, delta_t0: float) -> float

nodes.name: restore_original_split_order_for_tai
nodes.description: Promotes u1 and a2 to a common type and restores the original caller-specified split order: if the first input utc1 was the larger part (big1=true), tai1=u1 and tai2=a2; otherwise tai1=a2 and tai2=u1.

inputs.name: u1
inputs.constraints: larger-magnitude UTC JD part (first part in canonical form)

inputs.name: big1
inputs.constraints: true iff utc1 originally had larger magnitude

outputs.name: tai1
outputs.constraints: first part of two-part TAI Julian date, in original split order

outputs.name: tai2
outputs.constraints: second part of two-part TAI Julian date, in original split order

nodes.type_signature: (u1: float, a2: float, big1: bool) -> tuple[float, float]

nodes.name: tai_to_utc_inversion
nodes.description: Entry-point atom. Inverts the UTC→TAI mapping to recover UTC from a given TAI epoch. Uses an iterative bracketing strategy: seeds a candidate UTC estimate, calls the utc_to_tai_leap_second_kernel to evaluate the forward mapping, then refines until the residual is within floating-point tolerance.

inputs.name: tai1
inputs.type_desc: float — first part of two-part TAI Julian date
inputs.constraints: finite; must fall within the supported leap-second table range

inputs.name: tai2
inputs.type_desc: float — second part of two-part TAI Julian date

inputs.name: tai_estimate
inputs.type_desc: float pair — TAI value returned by utc_to_tai_leap_second_kernel for the candidate UTC; used to compute and reduce the residual
inputs.constraints: fed from utc_to_tai_leap_second_kernel output on each iteration

outputs.name: utc1
outputs.type_desc: float — first part of recovered two-part UTC Julian date
outputs.constraints: utc1 + utc2 + leap_seconds/86400 ≈ tai1 + tai2 within floating-point epsilon

outputs.name: utc2
outputs.type_desc: float — second part of recovered two-part UTC Julian date
outputs.constraints: precision-preserving companion to utc1

outputs.name: candidate_utc
outputs.type_desc: float pair — current UTC trial value forwarded to utc_to_tai_leap_second_kernel for evaluation
outputs.constraints: updated each iteration; becomes final utc1/utc2 on convergence

nodes.type_signature: (tai1: float — first part of two-part TAI Julian date, tai2: float — second part of two-part TAI Julian date, tai_estimate: float pair — TAI value returned by utc_to_tai_leap_second_kernel for the candidate UTC; used to compute and reduce the residual) -> tuple[float — first part of recovered two-part UTC Julian date, float — second part of recovered two-part UTC Julian date, float pair — current UTC trial value forwarded to utc_to_tai_leap_second_kernel for evaluation]
nodes.conceptual_summary: iterative_residual_inversion_of_tabulated_monotone_offset_bijection. Given a target scalar y encoded as a high-precision two-part float, and a forward mapping f(x) = x + δ(x) where δ(x) is a piecewise-constant step function defined over a lookup table, this atom iteratively refines a candidate pre-image x̂ by evaluating the residual ε = f(x̂) - y via the forward kernel and subtracting it from the current estimate until |ε| falls within floating-point epsilon. The algorithm exploits the near-identity structure of the offset (δ << x) to seed x̂₀ ≈ y, enabling rapid convergence—typically in one to two iterations—without requiring an analytical inverse. Precision is preserved throughout via a compensated two-part scalar representation that prevents catastrophic cancellation during residual computation.. Applications: Signal processing — recovering original sample timestamps from a non-uniformly resampled time axis when the resampling warp function is defined by a piecewise-constant interpolation table (e.g., inverting a frame-rate conversion map to align heterogeneous sensor streams), Thermodynamics / materials science — inverting a tabulated piecewise-linear enthalpy-to-temperature lookup (e.g., JANAF or NIST tables with discrete phase-transition discontinuities) to recover temperature from a measured enthalpy value with full double-precision fidelity, Geodesy / cartography — recovering geodetic latitude from projected northing in a tabulated map projection (e.g., transverse Mercator with discrete zonal corrections) where no closed-form inverse exists and a forward-evaluation residual loop achieves sub-millimeter accuracy, Pharmacokinetics / chronobiology — inverting a piecewise-constant clearance schedule to recover the precise dosing or event time corresponding to a measured plasma concentration, where the clearance function changes discretely at known physiological phase boundaries

nodes.name: order_tai_parts_by_magnitude
nodes.description: Determines which of the two TAI Julian date parts has greater absolute magnitude and reorders them so the larger part comes first, recording the original ordering for later restoration.

inputs.name: tai1
inputs.constraints: first part of two-part TAI Julian date

inputs.name: tai2
inputs.constraints: second part of two-part TAI Julian date

outputs.name: a1
outputs.constraints: larger-magnitude part of TAI date

outputs.name: a2
outputs.constraints: smaller-magnitude part of TAI date

outputs.name: big1
outputs.constraints: true if tai1 had the larger magnitude (used to restore original order)

nodes.type_signature: (tai1: float, tai2: float) -> tuple[float, float, bool]

nodes.name: seed_utc_estimate
nodes.description: Initialises the UTC trial value as a direct copy of the reordered TAI parts, providing a zeroth-order estimate before iterative refinement begins.

outputs.name: u1
outputs.constraints: initial UTC candidate, big part

outputs.name: u2
outputs.constraints: initial UTC candidate, small part

nodes.name: evaluate_and_refine_utc_estimate
nodes.description: Iteratively refines the UTC estimate by calling utc_to_tai_leap_second_kernel on the current candidate, computing the TAI residual (a1-g1, a2-g2), and applying the correction to u2. Runs for two iterations to converge through any leap-second boundary.

outputs.name: u1
outputs.constraints: refined UTC candidate, big part (unchanged)

outputs.name: u2
outputs.constraints: refined UTC candidate, small part, corrected by TAI residual

nodes.type_signature: (u1: float, u2: float, a1: float, a2: float) -> tuple[float, float]

nodes.name: restore_utc_output_order
nodes.description: Restores the UTC result to match the original input ordering of tai1/tai2, swapping u1 and u2 if the smaller TAI part was originally first, and type-promotes the pair for consistent return types.

inputs.name: big1
inputs.constraints: flag indicating whether tai1 was originally the larger part

outputs.name: utc1
outputs.constraints: first part of recovered UTC Julian date, mirroring tai1 slot

outputs.name: utc2
outputs.constraints: second part of recovered UTC Julian date, mirroring tai2 slot

nodes.type_signature: (u1: float, u2: float, big1: bool) -> tuple[float, float]
